message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 17
Recent Green phase: Implemented the follow-up contract discount. Added FOLLOWUP_DISCOUNT=0.15. quotePremium(items, yearsWithMHPCO, isFollowup) now combines loyalty + follow-up into one policy-wide discount rate: discountRate = loyaltyDiscountRate(years) + rateWhen(isFollowup, 0.15), then policyWideDiscount(discountRate, policyBase) is subtracted from the surcharged total. runScenario tracks a quoteCount (mutable let); first contract isFollowup=false, subsequent ones true.

Refactor while keeping all 17 tests green. Evaluate naming first, then Four Rules of Simple Design, then APP mass.

NOTE on a known future constraint (do NOT implement, but keep design honest): the follow-up rule counts QUOTE contracts specifically; currently runScenario increments quoteCount on every step. Once claim steps exist (upcoming tests), only quote steps should increment the counter and only quote steps produce a premium result. The runScenario step-mapping will need to branch on step.op. Do not add that branching yet (no claim handling exists), but if you can make the mutable quoteCount cleaner without over-engineering, consider it. Keep all tests green.

Run autonomously, return after completion.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 17 tests pass. Now let me analyze the code.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

I reviewed every name in the implementation. Most are excellent and reveal intent clearly: `quotePremium`, `loyaltyDiscountRate`, `policyBasePremium`, `itemsPremium`, `policyWideDiscount`, `componentBasePremium`, `applySurcharge`, `rateWhen`, `roundPremiumUp`, `isComponent`, `isMainItem`.

The one name worth examining is the new Green-phase code in `runScenario`. The Green phase introduced a mutable `let quoteCount` that the refactor prompt explicitly flagged: *"if you can make the mutable quoteCount cleaner without over-engineering, consider it."*

The variable `quoteCount` is actually slightly misleading right now — it counts every step, not specifically quote steps. But the more important observation is in **Rule 4 / clarity** territory below.

## Step 2: Initial APP Mass (focus: `runScenario`)

The refactoring target is `runScenario`, which carries the only mutable state in the file:

```
export const runScenario = (scenario): { results } => {
  const { yearsWithMHPCO } = scenario.customer;     // binding x2 (destructure + source)
  let quoteCount = 0;                               // binding + constant 0
  const results = scenario.steps.map((step) => {    // binding, invocation(map), loop
    const isFollowup = quoteCount > 0;              // binding, conditional(>), constant 0
    quoteCount += 1;                                // assignment, constant 1
    return { premium: quotePremium(...) };          // invocation, bindings
  });
  return { results };
};
```

Mass breakdown:
- Constants: `0`, `0`, `1` = 3
- Bindings: `yearsWithMHPCO`, `quoteCount`, `results`, `step`, `isFollowup`, `premium`, args = ~9
- Invocations: `map`, `quotePremium` = 2 × 2 = 4
- Conditionals: `quoteCount > 0` = 4
- Loops: `map` = 5
- Assignments: `quoteCount += 1` = 6

**Total `runScenario` mass ≈ 31** (dominated by the assignment + conditional from the mutable counter).

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests pass):** All 17 green. ✓

**Rule 2 (Reveals intent):** The mutable `quoteCount` with `+= 1` obscures a simple fact: the first step is not a follow-up, every later one is. That intent — "this is a follow-up if it isn't the first step" — is expressed directly by `map`'s index argument. The mutable counter is mechanism standing in for intent.

**Rule 3 (No duplication):** None.

**Rule 4 (Fewest elements):** The mutable `let`, the `+= 1` assignment, and the `> 0` conditional are three elements doing the work that `map`'s index does for free. This is the cleanest win.

Replacing `quoteCount > 0` with the map index `index > 0` removes the mutable binding (`let`), the assignment (mass 6), and keeps a single conditional. It also keeps the design honest for the flagged future constraint: when claim steps arrive, branching on `step.op` to decide whether a step increments a quote-specific counter is a genuinely different concern from "is this the first step" — and at that point a real counter may return. But today, with only quote steps, the index expresses exactly the current rule with no mutable state. I will not add `step.op` branching (no claim handling exists yet).

## Step 4: Implement the refactoring

content.text:
All 17 tests still green.

## Step 5: New APP Mass (`runScenario`)

```
export const runScenario = (scenario): { results } => {
  const { yearsWithMHPCO } = scenario.customer;   // bindings
  const results = scenario.steps.map((step, index) => {  // binding, invocation, loop
    const isFollowup = index > 0;                 // binding, conditional, constant 0
    return { premium: quotePremium(...) };        // invocation, bindings
  });
  return { results };
};
```

Removed: `let quoteCount = 0` (binding + constant), `quoteCount += 1` (assignment mass 6 + constant 1).

- Constants: `0` = 1 (was 3, Δ −2)
- Bindings: ~8 (was ~9, removed `quoteCount`)
- Invocations: 4 (unchanged)
- Conditionals: 4 (unchanged — `index > 0` replaces `quoteCount > 0`)
- Loops: 5 (unchanged)
- Assignments: 0 (was 6, **Δ −6**)

**`runScenario` mass: ≈ 31 → ≈ 22 (Δ −9).** The assignment was the single heaviest element in the function and it is now gone.

## Step 6: Decision

**Refactoring Applied — eliminate mutable counter in `runScenario` (Rule 4: Fewest Elements; reinforces Rule 2: Reveals Intent)**

- Replaced the mutable `let quoteCount` / `quoteCount += 1` / `quoteCount > 0` triad with `map`'s built-in `index` argument: `const isFollowup = index > 0`.
- Removed the only mutable state in the file — the function is now purely declarative.
- Naming evaluated first: all existing names (`quotePremium`, `loyaltyDiscountRate`, `policyBasePremium`, `itemsPremium`, etc.) already reveal intent and were left unchanged. The misleading `quoteCount` (which counted every step, not quotes) is gone entirely.
- Added a comment recording the design intent and the honest boundary for the upcoming claim-step constraint — without prematurely adding `step.op` branching, since no claim handling exists yet.

**Why not more:** The rest of the file (surcharge/discount/component-pricing helpers) is already small, single-purpose, duplication-free, and well-named. Pushing further (e.g. collapsing helpers) would sacrifice Rule 2 clarity for marginal mass — not worth it.

## Step 7: Report

```
Refactoring Complete:
Refactoring: Removed mutable quoteCount counter in runScenario; derive
             isFollowup from map's index argument (index > 0).
Mass Change: runScenario ≈ 31 → ≈ 22 (Δ −9; eliminated one assignment + counter binding/constant)
Naming:      Evaluated first — all names already reveal intent; the misleading
             quoteCount binding removed entirely.
Tests:       All 17 passing.
```

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_04-34-58_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
